questionText: She asked me, "Where are you going?"
options: She asked me where I was going.
She asked me where was I going.
She asked me where I am going.
She asked me where you were going.
explanation: In reported questions, the word order changes from question form to statement form (subject + verb). 'Where are you going?' becomes 'where I was going'. The pronoun changes from 'you' to 'I' and the verb tense backshifts from 'are going' to 'was going'.

questionText: He said, "Do you like coffee?"
options: He asked me if I liked coffee.
He asked me do I like coffee.
He asked me if I like coffee.
He asked me did I like coffee.
explanation: For yes/no questions in reported speech, we use 'if' or 'whether'. The word order becomes statement order (subject + verb). The verb backshifts from 'do you like' to 'I liked'.

questionText: They asked us, "What time does the train leave?"
options: They asked us what time the train left.
They asked us what time did the train leave.
They asked us what time the train leaves.
They asked us what time does the train leave.
explanation: In wh-questions, we keep the wh-word but change to statement word order (subject + verb). 'Does the train leave' becomes 'the train left' with backshift. The auxiliary 'does' is removed and the verb changes to past tense.

questionText: She asked me, "Are you coming to the party?"
options: She asked me if I was coming to the party.
She asked me if I am coming to the party.
She asked me was I coming to the party.
She asked me if you were coming to the party.
explanation: Reported yes/no question: use 'if' + statement word order. 'Are you coming' becomes 'if I was coming'. Pronoun changes from 'you' to 'I' and verb backshifts from 'are coming' to 'was coming'.

questionText: He asked, "What did you buy?"
options: He asked what I had bought.
He asked what did I buy.
He asked what I bought.
He asked what I had buy.
explanation: Past simple ('did you buy') backshifts to past perfect ('had bought') in reported speech. The word order becomes statement order: 'what I had bought'.

questionText: The teacher asked, "Who knows the answer?"
options: The teacher asked who knew the answer.
The teacher asked who knows the answer.
The teacher asked who did know the answer.
The teacher asked who know the answer.
explanation: When the wh-word is the subject of the question ('who'), we don't add an auxiliary or change the word order. We just backshift the verb from 'knows' to 'knew'.

questionText: She asked, "Why haven't you finished your homework?"
options: She asked why I hadn't finished my homework.
She asked why haven't I finished my homework.
She asked why I haven't finished my homework.
She asked why hadn't I finished my homework.
explanation: 'Why haven't you' becomes 'why I hadn't'. Present perfect ('haven't finished') backshifts to past perfect ('hadn't finished'). Pronouns change from 'you' to 'I' and from 'your' to 'my'.

questionText: They asked, "Can you speak French?"
options: They asked if I could speak French.
They asked if I can speak French.
They asked could I speak French.
They asked if I could speak French?
explanation: Modal verbs backshift: 'can' becomes 'could' in reported speech. For yes/no questions, use 'if' and statement word order: 'if I could speak French'.

questionText: She asked me, "Where have you been?"
options: She asked me where I had been.
She asked me where I have been.
She asked me where had I been.
She asked me where you had been.
explanation: Present perfect ('have been') backshifts to past perfect ('had been'). Wh-questions use statement word order: 'where I had been'. Pronoun 'you' changes to 'I'.

questionText: He asked, "Do you want to go out tonight?"
options: He asked if I wanted to go out that night.
He asked if I want to go out tonight.
He asked if I wanted to go out tonight.
He asked do I want to go out that night.
explanation: Time expression 'tonight' changes to 'that night' in reported speech. Verb backshifts: 'do you want' becomes 'if I wanted'. Pronoun 'you' changes to 'I'.

questionText: She asked, "What is your name?"
options: She asked what my name was.
She asked what was my name.
She asked what my name is.
She asked what is my name.
explanation: In reported wh-questions, the word order changes to statement order (subject + verb). 'What is your name?' becomes 'what my name was' with backshift from 'is' to 'was'.

questionText: They asked, "Did you see the accident?"
options: They asked if I had seen the accident.
They asked if I saw the accident.
They asked did I see the accident.
They asked if I had see the accident.
explanation: Past simple ('did you see') backshifts to past perfect ('had seen'). Yes/no questions use 'if' and statement word order: 'if I had seen the accident'.

questionText: He asked me, "Who will help you?"
options: He asked me who would help me.
He asked me who will help me.
He asked me who would help you.
He asked me who would help I.
explanation: 'Will' backshifts to 'would' in reported speech. The wh-word 'who' is the subject, so we don't change the word order. Pronoun 'you' changes to 'me'.

questionText: She asked, "Is John coming to the meeting?"
options: She asked if John was coming to the meeting.
She asked if John is coming to the meeting.
She asked was John coming to the meeting.
She asked if John came to the meeting.
explanation: Present continuous ('is coming') backshifts to past continuous ('was coming'). Yes/no questions use 'if' and statement word order: 'if John was coming to the meeting'.

questionText: They asked, "Where does your sister live?"
options: They asked where my sister lived.
They asked where did my sister live.
They asked where my sister lives.
They asked where does my sister live.
explanation: Present simple ('does your sister live') backshifts to past simple ('my sister lived'). The auxiliary 'does' is removed and verb becomes past tense. Pronoun 'your' changes to 'my'.

questionText: He asked, "What are you doing?"
options: He asked what I was doing.
He asked what am I doing.
He asked what I am doing.
He asked what was I doing.
explanation: Present continuous ('are you doing') backshifts to past continuous ('I was doing'). Wh-questions use statement word order and pronoun 'you' changes to 'I'.

questionText: She asked, "Do you have any money?"
options: She asked if I had any money.
She asked if I have any money.
She asked do I have any money.
She asked if I had some money.
explanation: Present simple ('do you have') backshifts to past simple ('I had'). Yes/no questions use 'if' and statement word order. The determiner 'any' stays the same in reported speech.

questionText: They asked, "How long have you been waiting?"
options: They asked how long I had been waiting.
They asked how long I have been waiting.
They asked how long had I been waiting.
They asked how long I had been wait.
explanation: Present perfect continuous ('have you been waiting') backshifts to past perfect continuous ('I had been waiting'). Wh-questions use statement word order and pronoun 'you' changes to 'I'.

questionText: He asked, "Could you help me with this?"
options: He asked if I could help him with that.
He asked if I can help him with that.
He asked could I help him with that.
He asked if I could help him with this.
explanation: 'Could' can remain 'could' in reported speech (it doesn't backshift). Yes/no questions use 'if' and statement word order. Pronoun 'you' changes to 'I', 'me' changes to 'him', and 'this' changes to 'that'.

questionText: She asked, "Which book did you choose?"
options: She asked which book I had chosen.
She asked which book I chose.
She asked which book did I choose.
She asked which book I had choose.
explanation: Past simple ('did you choose') backshifts to past perfect ('I had chosen'). Wh-questions use statement word order. The auxiliary 'did' is removed and verb becomes past participle.

questionText: They asked, "Will you be at the office tomorrow?"
options: They asked if I would be at the office the next day.
They asked if I will be at the office tomorrow.
They asked if I would be at the office tomorrow.
They asked will I be at the office the next day.
explanation: 'Will' backshifts to 'would'. 'Tomorrow' changes to 'the next day' in reported speech. Yes/no questions use 'if' and statement word order. Pronoun 'you' changes to 'I'.

questionText: He asked, "Why did she leave early?"
options: He asked why she had left early.
He asked why she left early.
He asked why did she leave early.
He asked why she had leave early.
explanation: Past simple ('did she leave') backshifts to past perfect ('she had left'). Wh-questions use statement word order. The auxiliary 'did' is removed and verb becomes past participle.

questionText: She asked, "What time is it?"
options: She asked what time it was.
She asked what time it is.
She asked what time was it.
She asked what time it were.
explanation: Present simple ('is') backshifts to past simple ('was'). Wh-questions use statement word order: 'what time it was'. The verb 'to be' becomes 'was' for third person singular.

questionText: They asked, "Have you seen my keys?"
options: They asked if I had seen their keys.
They asked if I have seen their keys.
They asked have I seen their keys.
They asked if I had seen my keys.
explanation: Present perfect ('have you seen') backshifts to past perfect ('I had seen'). Yes/no questions use 'if' and statement word order. Pronoun 'my' changes to 'their' in reported speech.

questionText: He asked, "Who did you meet at the conference?"
options: He asked who I had met at the conference.
He asked who I met at the conference.
He asked who did I meet at the conference.
He asked who I had meet at the conference.
explanation: Past simple ('did you meet') backshifts to past perfect ('I had met'). The wh-word 'who' is the object here, so we use statement word order. Pronoun 'you' changes to 'I'.

questionText: She asked, "Does your brother speak English?"
options: She asked if my brother spoke English.
She asked if my brother speaks English.
She asked does my brother speak English.
She asked if my brother had spoken English.
explanation: Present simple ('does your brother speak') backshifts to past simple ('my brother spoke'). Yes/no questions use 'if' and statement word order. The auxiliary 'does' is removed and verb becomes past tense.
